skill.name: supabase-postgres-best-practices
skill.description: Postgres performance optimization and best practices from Supabase. Use this skill when writing, reviewing, or optimizing Postgres queries, schema designs, or database configurations.
skill.summary: Comprehensive Postgres optimization guide with 26 rules across 8 categories, from query performance to security.
skill.author: supabase

security_audit.summary: All 710 static findings are false positives. This is a documentation-only skill containing Postgres best practices in Markdown format. The flagged patterns (backticks, MD5 references, URLs, system queries) are all legitimate SQL examples, documentation links, and monitoring queries. No executable code, no data exfiltration, no malicious intent detected.

medium_findings.title: False Positive - SQL Code Blocks Flagged as External Commands
medium_findings.description: Static scanner detected 344 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across Markdown files. Investigation confirms these are Markdown code blocks containing SQL examples (e.g., ```sql ... ```) and inline backticks for emphasis. No actual shell execution exists. Confidence: 0.98 - Direct evidence that backticks are Markdown syntax, not executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive - MD5 References Not Cryptographic Weakness
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 46 instances of 'Weak cryptographic algorithm (MD5)'. Investigation confirms these are: (1) MD5 checksums in documentation URLs (Supabase/PostgreSQL docs), (2) File integrity hashes in metadata.json, not actual cryptographic implementations. No security risk. Confidence: 0.95 - Clear evidence these are documentation references, not crypto code.

low_findings.title: False Positive - Hardcoded URLs Are Legitimate Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 72 'Hardcoded URL' instances. Investigation confirms all are legitimate documentation references to supabase.com/docs and postgresql.org/docs. No external data exfiltration, no malicious endpoints. Confidence: 0.99 - All URLs point to official Supabase/PostgreSQL documentation.

low_findings.title: False Positive - System Queries Are Legitimate Monitoring
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 158 'System reconnaissance' patterns. Investigation confirms these are standard PostgreSQL monitoring queries (pg_stat_activity, pg_class, pg_indexes) used for performance diagnostics. No reconnaissance activity. Confidence: 0.97 - Standard Postgres system catalog queries for DBA monitoring.

low_findings.title: False Positive - SQL WITH Clauses Not JavaScript with Statements
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 4 'with statement (deprecated)' patterns. Investigation confirms these are SQL Common Table Expressions (CTEs) using 'WITH' clause, not deprecated JavaScript 'with' statements. No scope confusion risk. Confidence: 0.99 - Clearly SQL syntax in Postgres documentation.

content.user_title: Optimize Postgres Performance with Expert Best Practices
content.value_statement: Slow queries and poor schema design plague Postgres applications. This skill provides 26 prioritized rules from Supabase experts to achieve 10-1000x performance improvements.
actual_capabilities: Provides 26 categorized rules for Postgres performance optimization across query design, indexing, and schema
Offers incorrect vs. correct SQL examples with performance metrics (10-1000x improvements documented)
Covers connection management, Row-Level Security (RLS), and monitoring with specific configuration guidance
Includes EXPLAIN output analysis and query plan diagnostics for slow query troubleshooting
Delivers Supabase-specific recommendations for multi-tenant applications and managed Postgres environments
limitations: Documentation-only skill with no executable code or automated optimization tools
Rules require manual application to your database schema and queries
Performance improvements depend on your specific data distribution and access patterns
Does not replace professional database tuning for complex or high-scale scenarios

use_cases.title: Query Performance Troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Developer experiencing slow API endpoints uses query optimization rules to add indexes and rewrite queries for 100-1000x improvement.

use_cases.title: Database Schema Design Review
use_cases.description: Database architect reviews schema design rules before launching multi-tenant SaaS application to avoid costly refactoring.

use_cases.title: Postgres Migration Planning
use_cases.description: DevOps engineer plans migration from single-tenant to multi-tenant architecture using RLS and connection pooling guidelines.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Query Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt:
I have a slow Postgres query. Help me optimize it using best practices from the supabase-postgres-best-practices skill.

My query:
```sql
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 123 AND status = 'pending'
```

Table has 10 million rows. Query takes 5 seconds.
prompt_templates.scenario: New user needs help with first query optimization

prompt_templates.title: Index Strategy Review
prompt_templates.prompt:
Review my indexing strategy for this schema using the supabase-postgres-best-practices skill. Focus on composite indexes, partial indexes, and foreign key indexing.

Schema:
- users table (1M rows)
- orders table (5M rows, foreign key to users)
- Query pattern:经常 filter by user_id + created_at + status
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user designing multi-column index strategy

prompt_templates.title: RLS Policy Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt:
I'm implementing Row-Level Security for multi-tenant data using supabase-postgres-best-practices. Help me optimize RLS policies.

Current policy:
```sql
CREATE POLICY user_isolation ON documents
  USING (auth.uid() = user_id)
  WITH CHECK (auth.uid() = user_id);
```

Query performance degraded 5x after enabling RLS.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user working with Supabase RLS at scale

prompt_templates.title: Connection Pooling Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt:
Help me configure connection pooling for a Node.js application with Supabase using supabase-postgres-best-practices.

Requirements:
- 1000 concurrent users
- Average query time: 50ms
- Using PgBouncer
- Experiencing connection exhaustion errors

Provide specific configuration values and explain trade-offs.

output_examples.input:
Review my Postgres query and suggest optimizations based on best practices:

SELECT * FROM events WHERE user_id = 123 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20
output_examples.output:
Based on query-missing-indexes.md rules, your query needs an index on the filter and order columns:

1. Add composite index:
```sql
CREATE INDEX events_user_id_created_at_idx 
ON events (user_id, created_at DESC);
```

2. Expected improvement: Index scan reduces cost from 25,000 to 8.44 (100-1000x faster on large tables).

3. For LIMIT queries, consider usingcovering indexes to avoid table lookups if you only need specific columns.

No, this violates data-batch-inserts.md rules. You have:

- 1000 individual round trips
- 1000 separate transactions
- Estimated 10-50x slower than necessary

Correct approach (from rules):
```sql
INSERT INTO logs (message) VALUES
  ($1), ($2), ($3), ... up to ~1000 rows;
```

Or use COPY for bulk imports (fastest method). See data-batch-inserts.md for examples.

output_examples.input: Should I add an index on this foreign key column that gets queried occasionally?
output_examples.output:
Yes, absolutely. From schema-foreign-key-indexes.md:

**Impact: CRITICAL (5-20x improvement)**

Foreign key columns should almost always be indexed because:
1. JOINs on foreign keys cause sequential scans without indexes
2. DELETEs on parent tables require scanning child tables
3. Cascade operations become exponentially slower on large tables

The rule notes: Even occasional queries benefit from indexing foreign keys due to exponential scan costs.

best_practices: Always create indexes on WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY columns before deploying to production
Use cursor-based pagination with indexed columns instead of OFFSET for large result sets
Keep transactions short (under 1 second) and avoid user interaction mid-transaction to prevent lock contention
anti_patterns: Using SELECT * on large tables when only specific columns are needed (causes unnecessary I/O and prevents covering index optimization)
Running individual INSERT statements inside loops instead of batching rows or using COPY
Creating indexes without analyzing query patterns with EXPLAIN ANALYZE (some indexes may hurt write performance without helping reads)

faq.question: How do I know which columns need indexes?
faq.answer: Use the missing indexes query from query-missing-indexes.md to identify frequently accessed columns without indexes. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries to spot sequential scans. Prioritize WHERE and JOIN columns used in application-critical queries.

faq.question: Should I index every foreign key?
faq.answer: Almost always yes. According to schema-foreign-key-indexes.md, foreign key indexes provide 5-20x improvement for JOINs and prevent cascading DELETE performance issues. Only skip if the column is never queried or joined.

faq.question: Why is cursor-based pagination better than OFFSET?
faq.answer: OFFSET requires scanning and discarding all previous rows, becoming slower with deeper pagination. Cursor-based pagination (using indexed WHERE clauses) has O(1) performance regardless of page depth. See data-pagination.md for implementation examples.

faq.question: How many connection poolers do I need for Supabase?
faq.answer: From conn-pooling.md: Use PgBouncer in transaction mode. Pool size should be (number of application servers × connections per server). For 1000 concurrent users with 50ms queries, start with pool size of 20-50 and monitor for exhaustion errors.

faq.question: Does Row-Level Security (RLS) impact performance?
faq.answer: Yes, RLS can add 2-5x overhead if policies are suboptimal. security-rls-performance.md explains to use indexes on policy columns, avoid subqueries in policies, and use security_invoker views. Properly tuned RLS policies maintain security with minimal performance cost.

faq.question: Can I use these rules with raw PostgreSQL or only Supabase?
faq.answer: All core rules apply to raw PostgreSQL. Supabase-specific notes (connection pooling defaults, managed RLS) are clearly marked. The indexing, query optimization, and schema design rules work identically in any Postgres 14+ deployment.
